steps.title: Pin down Muller and Harris's claim
steps.content: Their specific conclusion is that giraffes "may even help each other care for one another's newborns"—i.e., adults cooperatively caring for calves that are not their own offspring.

steps.title: Define what would support it
steps.content: Direct support requires evidence of an adult giraffe providing care (feeding, protection, etc.) to a calf that is not her own. Evidence about solo parental behavior, aggression, or general communication does not address cooperative cross-family care.

steps.title: Eliminate by misread/overreach
steps.content:
• Choice A — Female giraffes have been observed feeding young giraffes that aren't their direct offspring.: precisely matches the claim—adults caring for others' young. ✓
  • Choice B — Confrontations between a younger and an older male giraffe are frequently observed.: describes aggression, the opposite of cooperative caregiving. ✗
  • Choice C — Some female giraffes have been observed sniffing and licking their newborn offspring.: describes normal mother-to-own-calf bonding; says nothing about helping other mothers. ✗
  • Choice D — Giraffes are able to make sounds but are rarely observed communicating with others.: would weaken sociality, not support cooperative care. ✗
A is correct.
